original_hypothesis.title: claimAuction Silent Refund Failures Lock Losing Bidders' ETH
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In claimAuction, ETH refunds to losing bidders use .call() without checking the return value. If a losing bidder's address is a contract that reverts on receive, their refund fails silently. The bid status is never set to false during the refund loop, but auctionClaim is set to true, preventing re-execution. The bidder's ETH is permanently locked.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Similar to payArtist, unchecked .call returns in the refund loop mean failed refunds result in permanent loss. Unlike cancelBid (which the bidder controls), this is the ONLY refund path post-auction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool success, ) = payable(auctionInfoData[_tokenid][i].bidder).call{value: auctionInfoData[_tokenid][i].bid}(""); without require(success); bid status never set to false

preconditions: Losing bidder bid from a contract that reverts on ETH receive
Auction ends and claimAuction is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ETH refund via .call without checking success; no alternative refund path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Failed refund is only emitted as event; bid status stays true but auctionClaim prevents retry
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pull-pattern for refunds, no success check, no retry mechanism

analysis_questions: Could a bidder who bid from a smart wallet (Gnosis Safe) face this issue?
Is there any way for the bidder to recover funds after failed refund?
Does the failed refund ETH stay in the contract recoverable by anyone?
validation_requirements: Bid from a contract that reverts on receive
Wait for auction end and claim
Verify ETH stuck in contract with no recovery path

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User bids from a smart contract wallet that has a gas-limited or reverting fallback. When claimAuction processes refunds, the transfer fails. The user's ETH is permanently locked in AuctionDemo with no recovery mechanism.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of individual bidder's ETH
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any bidder using a smart contract wallet
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - no retry or pull mechanism exists

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for failed ETH refunds during claim

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: success=false in Refund event, but ETH irrecoverable

validation_result.mechanism: unchecked ETH refund with no recovery path
validation_result.impact_summary: Losing bidders who bid from contracts that revert on ETH receive permanently lose their funds. auctionClaim prevents re-execution and cancelBid is blocked after auction ends, leaving no recovery mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The claimAuction function in AuctionDemo.sol processes refunds to losing bidders using low-level .call() without checking the return value. When a losing bidder is a contract that reverts on ETH receive (e.g., a contract without a receive/fallback function, or one that intentionally reverts), the refund fails silently. The critical issue is the combination of three factors: (1) the .call() success return is ignored, (2) the bid's status field is never set to false during the refund loop, and (3) auctionClaim[_tokenid] is set to true before the loop, preventing any re-execution. Additionally, cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime, making it unavailable post-auction. There is no emergency withdrawal function in AuctionDemo.sol. This means the failed refund ETH is permanently locked in the contract with absolutely no recovery path.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal accurately identifies the vulnerable pattern. Lines in the claimAuction function's else-if branch show: (bool success, ) = payable(auctionInfoData[_tokenid][i].bidder).call{value: auctionInfoData[_tokenid][i].bid}(""); with no require(success) and no status = false assignment. The auctionClaim flag set before the loop at auctionClaim[_tokenid] = true prevents retry.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The .call() refund to losing bidders does not check the success return value. If the bidder is a contract that reverts on receive, the refund silently fails. The bid status is never set to false, but auctionClaim is already set to true, preventing any retry. cancelBid is blocked post-auction. No emergency withdrawal exists in AuctionDemo.
cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime, so it cannot be used as a recovery mechanism after the auction ends. This eliminates the only other potential refund path for losing bidders.

calling_sequence: 1. WinnerOrAdminRequired modifier verifies caller is highest bidder or admin
2. require checks: block.timestamp >= auctionEndTime, auctionClaim == false, auctionStatus == true
5. Loop iterates all bids in auctionInfoData[_tokenid]
6. For winning bid: safeTransferFrom NFT + pay owner
7. For losing bids with status==true: .call() refund WITHOUT require(success)
8. If .call() fails (bidder is reverting contract), success=false is only emitted in event
9. Bid status remains true but auctionClaim prevents retry
10. ETH permanently locked - no cancelBid (auction ended), no emergency withdraw in AuctionDemo
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: auctionClaim set to true (line ~106) -> loop processes refunds (line ~112-116) -> .call() to losing bidder fails silently -> bid status stays true -> function completes -> auctionClaim=true blocks any re-call -> cancelBid blocked by timestamp check -> no emergency withdrawal function exists in AuctionDemo -> ETH permanently locked in contract

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for AuctionDemo. The fixturesDeployment.js does not deploy AuctionDemo, but the vulnerability is inherent in the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A user bids on an auction using a smart contract wallet that has a reverting fallback/receive function (or a contract deployed without receive/fallback). When the auction ends and claimAuction is called, the refund .call() to this bidder returns success=false. The function continues execution without reverting. After the loop completes, auctionClaim[_tokenid] is permanently true. The bidder cannot call cancelBid (requires timestamp <= endTime, which fails post-auction). There is no emergency withdraw function in AuctionDemo. The ETH is permanently locked. This is realistic because: (1) smart contract wallets like Gnosis Safe with custom guard modules could revert, (2) contracts without receive/fallback functions exist, (3) the scenario requires no special attacker - just an unfortunate bidder using a contract address.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any losing bidder who participated from a contract that cannot receive ETH will permanently lose their bid amount. This could affect users of smart contract wallets, multi-sig wallets with custom guards, or any contract-based bidder. The impact scales with bid size - there is no upper bound on the amount that could be locked. The ETH remains in the AuctionDemo contract with no recovery mechanism, as the contract has no emergency withdrawal function and no pull-pattern for refunds.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(success) after each .call() in the refund loop to ensure all refunds succeed or the transaction reverts (though this introduces DoS risk from malicious bidders)
Implement a pull-pattern for refunds: instead of pushing ETH in claimAuction, record pending refunds and let bidders withdraw via a separate function
Add a failedRefund mapping and a retryRefund function that allows re-attempting failed refunds
Add an emergency withdrawal function to AuctionDemo that can recover stuck ETH
Set bid status to false during the refund loop regardless of transfer success, and maintain a separate pendingWithdrawals mapping for failed refunds
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AuctionDemo with minter, gencore, admins contracts. 2. Deploy a RevertingBidder contract that has no receive/fallback (or one that reverts). 3. Mint a token via mintAndAuction. 4. Have RevertingBidder call participateToAuction with some ETH. 5. Have an EOA bid higher. 6. Advance time past auctionEndTime. 7. Call claimAuction. 8. Verify RevertingBidder's ETH is stuck in AuctionDemo contract. 9. Verify cancelBid reverts with 'Auction ended'. 10. Verify claimAuction reverts (auctionClaim already true). 11. Confirm no recovery path exists.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers may vary slightly depending on how the source is counted (comments, blank lines), but the vulnerable pattern is clearly present in the claimAuction function
Gnosis Safe specifically may handle ETH receives correctly in most configurations, but custom guard modules or non-standard contract wallets could trigger this
additional_testing_needed: Test with various contract wallet implementations (Gnosis Safe, Argent, custom multi-sigs) to determine real-world prevalence
Test whether the Refund event with success=false is sufficient for off-chain monitoring to detect the issue
Verify that no admin-level workaround exists (e.g., can the admin extract locked ETH through any indirect means)
